title: Immigration Policies Proposed in the Major Party Platforms for the 2024 US Presidential Election
abstract: Immigration policy is a major campaign topic in the 2024 presidential election in the United States, given salience by the masses of irregular economic migrants and asylum-seekers crossing the US-Mexico border. (A Pew survey of January 2024 found almost 80 percent of American adults agreeing that the numbers seeking entry at the southern border constituted a crisis or major problem.) A related issue in contention is how to deal with the large undocumented population living in the country-estimated to amount to 11 million, half of them visa overstayers and another segment, dreamers, brought illegally to the United States as children. Most could be technically liable to deportation. The former Trump administration (2017-2020) and the current Biden administration have had sharply divergent views on immigration policy-generally restrictive in the former case, cautiously more welcoming in the latter. (Insistent public pressure, political realities, and the exigencies of border management, however, lessened the differences in practical outcomes of the two administrations.) Looking ahead, the earlier policy divergence is renewed and even accentuated in the platforms of the two major political parties for the 2024 election. Relevant sections of each platform are reproduced below. The Republican Party platform is as cursory on immigration as on other policy areas-notably in its cryptic mention of beginning the largest deportation program in US history. The platform's tone is set by the reference in the title to the migrant invasion. The Democratic Party platform, drafted before the replacement of Joseph Biden with Kamala Harris as the Party's designated presidential nominee(and not subsequently revised to remove references to a second Biden administration), is anything but cursory: it is six times the length. It promises stronger border enforcement and faster processing of asylum claims, together with more generous refugee admissions and a larger, robust legal immigration system. It continues to push for a pathway to citizenship for Dreamers and for other long-term undocumented individuals who have been working and paying taxes, provisions requiring Congressional action.
